issue_title: RTL-TCP gains adjustments
issue_body:
Hi,

Tested RTL-TCP successfully this evening with URH 1.6.1.
Here are my comments about gain values for this particular device.
RTL-SDR v3 dongle is used on my rtl-tcp server.

Here is a rtl-tcp output with SDR# client connected:
> Use the device argument 'rtl_tcp=x.x.x.x:1234' in OsmoSDR (gr-osmosdr) source
> to receive samples in GRC and control rtl_tcp parameters (frequency, gain, ...).
> client accepted!
> set sample rate 900001
> Exact sample rate is: 900001.019241 Hz
> set freq correction 0
> set freq 123456789
> set agc mode 0
> set gain mode 1
> set tuner gain by index 17
> 

And here is a rtl-tcp output with URH client connected with gain value 50 in signal recorder :
> Use the device argument 'rtl_tcp=x.x.x.x:1234' in OsmoSDR (gr-osmosdr) source
> to receive samples in GRC and control rtl_tcp parameters (frequency, gain, ...).
> client accepted!
> set freq 123456789
> set sample rate 900001
> Exact sample rate is: 900001.019241 Hz
> set gain 50
> set freq correction 0
> set direct sampling 0
> Disabled direct sampling mode

Gain mode and values between two clients aren't the same
SDR# seems to use predefined gain values by giving their indexes.

Spectrum analyzer gives pretty accurate output when high gain values are selected (x10 in the rtl-tcp output). Using 34 for gain instead of 32 gives too much significant changes on spectrum. This have to be more progressive (< 32 : noise level only, > 34 signals are seen really strong above noise level)

I listen to frequencies where trunk messages are received every 30 seconds and signal recorder dosen't catch them at all while SDR# shows me those pulses by 25dB above noise level.

Furthermore signal recorder doesn't apply the x10 factor to gain values like spectrum analyzer does (it may be the reason).

Anyway thank you for improvements and support for rtl-tcp last week. Things go impressively fast on this project :)

issue_title: Automatically refresh static assets/resources
issue_body: For the notebook, static assets/resources (like JavaScript files) are installed with `notebook.nbextensions.install_nbextension`. When the assets are updated (often during development, for the user upon a new release) the browser still has them cached and a "forced" refresh is needed. This should happen automatically for new releases and caching should be switched off during development.

issue_title: How would you feel about pytoml becoming a dependency of pip?
issue_body:
Hello!

There's a discussion happening on distutils-sig about choosing a new standard configuration file format for Python source trees, to be read by tools like `pip` when they want to build a package. There are multiple threads, but maybe [this one](http://thread.gmane.org/gmane.comp.python.distutils.devel/25850) is the most relevant.

Currently sentiment seems to be shifting towards TOML, and attention is focused on pytoml because in some initial testing we found that pytoml seems to handle unicode correctly on py2 and [the other toml parser we could find](https://github.com/uiri/toml) doesn't.

So... I wanted to reach out to you and see what you thought about this. I'm super excited about the possibility of having something like TOML become a standard part of our package building toolkit. But... this would also mean a sudden huge influx of users and attention on your little project here, which I know can be a very mixed blessing. And there'd be issues that come up, like, pip needs support for python 2.6 and 3.3 -- I don't know if you're actually interested in supporting those going forward? (I actually have a patch for this that I'll submit as a PR in a moment, but there are also ongoing costs to keeping old python versions working and a PR doesn't solve that.) And we'd probably need to fix the [error message situation](https://github.com/avakar/pytoml/issues/5#issuecomment-121385235). And so forth.

What do you think?

issue_title: Implement some way to at least warn about the system packages required by the HDL packages
issue_body:
The example in the README, `wb_sdram_ctrl`, actually requires development version of `libelf` (`libelf-dev` on Ubuntu) but if you don't have that, you will only see:

```ERROR: Failed to build simulation model
ERROR: Failed to compile VPI library elf-loader```

In an e-mail discussion with @olofk he mentions he is aware of that but hasn't yet figured out a good way to handle this kind of stuff - since this is not a FuseSoC requirement per se - so I proposed an issue to initiate the discussion.

The least we could do of course would be to enable some messages to be printed by the package at build (like: `This package requires the development version of libelf, which may be libelf-dev on your system. Make sure you have it installed.`). I use that in our buildsystems where it is not trivial to explicitly check for something: of course this is not the best idea, but at least the developer would not need to bother people with questions if he can spot what's wrong himself.

Just writing it down in want of other propositions so far, I am sure there are better ways to handle it. Just thinking that actually having an example that can fail without a unambiguous warning/error message could potentially stop people who do 'let's download this and see what it is - not working - not useful' from giving FuseSoC a try. I happen to do exactly that if I'm not determined, the bad person I am ;)

issue_title: Exhaustive server pool is too exhaustive
issue_body:
When using a server pool with `active=True` and `exhaust=True`, if an LDAP server goes offline, it is never able to be used again. In a case where there may only be 2 servers in the pool, this is really detrimental to a stateful app, and requires the app to be restarted in order to re-negotiate connections to these LDAP servers. 

To try combat this, I have attempted to use `exhaust=False`. With the server offline, I attempt to make an LDAP connection, however the pool ends up in a loop attempting to find an active server. The loop never breaks when the server comes back online, and leads to an endless loop of polling.

issue_title: response_to_ldif creates false LDIF
issue_body:
I have made my search query with "conn.response_to_ldif()" as LDIF. Doing so, I realized that there is no empty line among the "LDAP entries". An import via ActiveDirectoryStudio or parsing with LdiParser (python-ldap) ends with error.

I found out that the function in rfc2849.py "search_response_to_ldif" inserts a line, but this line is removed by the function "operation_to_ldif".

Removing line "if line:" at the end of the function "operation_to_ldif" brought the desired result.

But it makes the gpx file in its entirety inconsistent, users have to remember that they also need to adjust waypoints and maybe other places where there are times.

It is also inconsistent within the API: gpx.move will move tracks, routes, waypoints - so why shouldn't adjust_time also adjust all of them?

Changing that would be an incompatible change but I still think it would be a good idea.

issue_title: add case to autoconf
issue_body:
Some projects encourage configuration to be done in OS environment variables or env files. In this case, the configuration keys are all uppercased like `NAMESPACE_KEY`.

Some projects encourage configuration to be done with INI files. In this case, configuration keys are more naturally expressed as lowercased like:

```
[namespace]
key=foo
```

I think it's prudent for the documentation to reflect the project's preferences.

As such, I want to add a `:case:` argument with options `upper`, `lower` and default to `lower`.

Hey Erin!

Trying to install the master branch of fitsio threw the following error:
```
python setup.py install
[snip]
creating build/lib.macosx-10.10-x86_64-2.7
creating build/lib.macosx-10.10-x86_64-2.7/fitsio

running build_ext
building 'fitsio._fitsio_wrap' extension
gcc -fno-strict-aliasing -fno-common -dynamic -g -O2 -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -I/usr/local/include -I/usr/local/opt/sqlite/include -I/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/include/python2.7 -c fitsio/fitsio_pywrap.c -o build/temp.macosx-10.10-x86_64-2.7/fitsio/fitsio_pywrap.o -arch x86_64

I solved this by changing the following lines of setup.py from

ext=Extension("fitsio._fitsio_wrap",

              extra_objects=extra_objects,
              extra_compile_args=extra_compile_args, 
              extra_link_args=extra_link_args)
```
to
```
ext=Extension("fitsio._fitsio_wrap", 
              sources,
              extra_objects=extra_objects,
              extra_compile_args=extra_compile_args, 
              extra_link_args=extra_link_args,
              include_dirs=[cfitsio_dir,numpy.get_include()])
```
and everything built properly.  I can submit a PR if you'd like.

issue_title: small error in cat()
issue_body:
I'm concatenating bedtools with bedtool.cat(*[bedtools]) and it works fine, except that everything gets truncated to BED3 even with force_truncate=False and postmerge=False.

I tracked down the issue to a small mistake in cat() (at bedtool.py line 2690):

field_nums = set([i.field_count for i in other_beds]).difference([None])

Turns out that i.field_count doesn't return the count, it returns the method. It should be field_count() instead. Also you might want to add [self.field_count()] just as the type. So the final would be:

field_nums = set(self.field_count() + [i.field_count() for i in other_beds]).difference([None])

Thanks,
Michael

issue_body:
Hi!

per_chat_id() only checks for a chat id in msg['chat']['id'], while for callback queries (as specified in https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#callbackquery and seen in the wild), the corresponding chat id is going to be in msg['message']['chat']['id']. (at least for "normal", not inline messages)

Cheers, 

Michael

issue_title: Suggestion: namedtuples for entries
issue_body:
Thanks for creating this library. I've a suggestion for improvement: use named tuples for entries rather than plain tuples. Here's an example:

    from collections import namedtuple
    Entry = namedtuple('Entry', ['start', 'end', 'label'])
    
    # Example
    entry = Entry(0.0, 0.30, 'sound')
    print(entry)

Result: `Entry(start=0.0, end=0.3, label='sound')`

The nice thing about this is that you don't have to do any indexing anymore, but you can just do `entry.start` to get the starting time.

issue_title: CronExpression does not accept <unicode> type
issue_body:
# Minimum Code
```python
from cronex import CronExpression
ce = CronExpression(u'* * * * *')
```

# Expected result
Code should run without issue

# Observed Result
Raises error as
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/user/.python-virtualenvs/codebase/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cronex/__init__.py", line 107, in __init__
    self.string_tab = map(str.upper, [minutes, hours, dom, months, dow])
TypeError: descriptor 'upper' requires a 'str' object but received a 'unicode'
```

# Workaround
Simply convert your `unicode` to `str` for the time being
```python
from cronex import CronExpression
ce = CronExpression(u'* * * * *'.encode('utf-8))
```

If you are unsure if the object is of type `unicode` or `str`, you can still use the same method

```python
# `my_string` is defined elsewhere, it is possibly a <unicode> or <str> object
from cronex import CronExpression
ce = CronExpression(my_string.encode('utf-8'))
```

issue_title: Feature request: get indices of nearby points
issue_body:
Say we create a tree, TREE, from two arrays, RA and DEC.  I think it would be useful to have a method "query_point" that returns the indices of points in the RA and DEC arrays that are within a given distance from the queried point.  So, for instance, one could do:

```
RA0 = 90.
DEC0 = 0.
radius = 10.
indices = TREE.query_point(RA0, DEC0, radius)
RA_nearby, DEC_nearby = RA[indices], DEC[indices]
```

This is exactly analogous to the "query_ball_point" method in scipy.spatial.KDTree: http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy-0.14.0/reference/generated/scipy.spatial.KDTree.query_ball_point.html#scipy.spatial.KDTree.query_ball_point.  

Thanks!

issue_body:

issue_title: Binary pixel stream is detected as text
issue_body:
This is originally from https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/issues/50
In this archive https://rubygems.org/downloads/chunky_png-1.2.8.gem the file:
chunky_png-1.2.8.gem-extract/data.tar.gz-extract/spec/resources/pixelstream.rgb
is detected as text even though this is clearly a binary data stream.

libmagic file detects it as octet/stream or data which is correct (for instance with this libmagic ctypes binding: https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/blob/master/src/typecode/magic2.py )

The issue comes from the fact that bytes above 127 are treated as text, and the test file first 1024 bytes are FF. The original Perl binary detection looked for bytes below 127 only and did some extra stuffs.

issue_title: Global completion doesn't work for pytest
issue_body:
[pytest](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest) has its command line scripts installed via [setuptools.setup(entry_points={'console_scripts': ...})](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/master/setup.py#L69) construction. If I understand correctly, argcomplete's global completion should handle this case and work just fine in such a situation, but it doesn't.
Looks like [this if statement](https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete/blob/master/argcomplete/bash_completion.d/python-argcomplete.sh#L49) should cover such a case, but `pytest` script created after installation doesn't have any of these strings in it. Here is the full content of `pytest` script from my environment:
```
#!/Users/pavel/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/envs/test_env/bin/python3.6

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import re
import sys

from pytest import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r'(-script\.pyw?|\.exe)?$', '', sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(main())
```
`pytest` module from which `main` function is imported has `# PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK` as its first string.

I've opened a ticket in pytest project (see https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/2725), but it looks like that is a problem of argcomplete.

issue_title: strategy_for_instances will behave arbitrarily with diamond inheritance
issue_body:
Suppose you have types A, B with instance definitions for both, and C subtyping both A and B. An instance of C will basically get an arbitrary strategy - it'll be stable across runs within a single process because of dict iteration order but will change from process to process.

Really this needs to be an error condition. If you have diamond inheritance you need to provide a more specific implementation for the more specific type.

issue_title: django-crontab logging doesn't appear to use the default timezone
issue_body:
Hi @kraiz, thanks for your work on this project.  I've been using django-crontab for the last year, it's simple and pretty much Just Works.  However I've noticed recently that django-crontab logging doesn't appear to use the default timezone.

settings.py
```
TIME_ZONE = 'America/Toronto' # -05:00 EST
USE_TZ = True
CRONJOBS = [
    ('0 9 * * *', 'myapp.cron.daily_order_summary', '>> cron.log'),
]
```

cron.py
```
from django.utils import timezone

def daily_order_summary():
    now = timezone.now()
    print('running daily_order_summary ({})'.format(now))
```

cron.log
```
running daily_order_summary (2017-01-11 14:00:03.304917+00:00)
```

I was expecting cron.log to be
```
running daily_order_summary (2017-01-11 09:00:03.304917-05:00)
```

Here's my server time a few minutes after running the above cron, note the "EST"
```
django@host:~$ date
Wed Jan 11 09:03:39 EST 2017
```

I'm running
- ubuntu 14.04
- django 1.8.x
- django-crontab (0.7.1)

To be clear, the cron job _runs at the correct time_, but the logging output for some reason isn't displaying the correct timezone.

issue_title: Why does zobrist_hash ignore legality of en passant captures?
issue_body:
I got stung recently by the fact that the following two variations:

    1.Nf3 Nf6 2.c4 Nc6 3.Qb3 e5 4.Qe3 e4 5.d4
    1.c4 e5 2.d4 Nc6 3.Qd3 Nf6 4.Nf3 e4 5.Qe3

though they have identical `epd`s, have different `zobrist_hash`es. The reason is that for the first variation, the computation of the hash uses the position's `ep_square`, even though the en passant capture is illegal. (Black's e pawn is pinned.) I want these two variations to count as transpositions of each other, so I needed to redefine the `zobrist_hash` function to take account of the en passant square only when the e.p. capture is legal.

What's the justification for the existing behavior? Just a small performance boost?

issue_title: Possibility to pass an explicit list of bins to `df.iplot(kind='hist', ...)`
issue_body:
See [this question on SE](https://stackoverflow.com/q/45784591/4063051) for major details.

Basically, it seems that the `bins` parameter only accepts an integer, to be interpreted as the total number of bins in the plot. This is a problem when plotting multiple histograms with different data ranges because they get binned indipendently and thus often inconsistently (see above post).

This seems to be confirmed in (what seems to me to be) the [relevant section of the code](https://github.com/santosjorge/cufflinks/blob/ed3e666a017c869ef1c85a05dcdae5e222509b5e/cufflinks/plotlytools.py#L803-L834).

Is there another intended way to explicitly set the bins, like can be done in `matplotlib`/`pandas` directly with the `bins` parameter, or in `plotly` with `xbins`?

issue_title: Support disabling pylint via the command line
issue_body:
Hey, thank you for this package! I have a small feature request - we add `--pylint` to our `setup.cfg` file so that it is always run alongside our tests:

```
[tool:pytest]
addopts = -p no:cacheprovider -n auto --junitxml /tmp/unit_results.xml --pylint
```

This works fine, but I find myself wishing to just run our tests locally without the linting (which is a bit faster). 

I'd love the ability to run `pytest --no-pylint` and have it override the auto-included `--pylint` flag, so that `pytest --pylint --no-pylint` will not run pylint.

issue_title: Templates with empty name
how to recover from an intermittent TCP timeout exception
issue_body:
What is the proper way to recover from a snap7 exception ISO : An error occurred during recv TCP : Connection timed out?

If I place a db_read() into a try/except clause to keep the application from aborting on TCP timeout, subsequent db_read() become out of sync.  It seems *somewhere* the calls are queued.  Probably in the native snap7 thread.

For example: If I have three locations I am reading from a PLC 
3,260,4 contains value 100
3,272,4 contains value 200
1,104,4 contains value 300

-the first db_read(3,260,4) call successfully returns a value 100
-second db_read(3,272,4) call times out with TCP timeout exception 
-third db_read(1,104,4) call will return value 200 (Not 300 like you would expect)
..
-a fourth call of db_read(3,260,4) will return the value from the third call above (300)

It seems the method calls get spooled into a queue and for every call that times out, the data returned will be delayed that many calls in the future.  

Is there a way to throw away the buffer on a tcp timeout?  Or is there some other philosophical approach I should be taking?  It seems very dangerous to call a function with specific arguments and get the wrong data back.  Thanks for any suggestions.

issue_title: Release v0.2.5 update
issue_body:
Since v0.2.4, a handful of small and large updates have been pulled into the project. `bulkwalk` is the most notable addition with memory leak patches following just behind it.

While this series of tests is overkill, I want to be *very certain* that my changes will not break existing code, nor introduce additional leaks. Python 3 behavior appears to be fine, however Python 2 seems to still have some odd reports — of which I cannot determine if it is normal or not. This is all to ensure that we will not break anyone's projects that are in production.

Due to some of the code refactoring, and the addition of OS X/macOS `brew` support, I would like to perform some intensive and thorough tests before proceeding:

Platform | Py Version | Test | Description
---------|------------|-----|------------
Mint 18 | 2.7.x | Memory Leak Checks | Frequent equipment polling will take place. No manual GC calls. 4GB RAM will be dedicated to the system. Run for 48 hours; check memory usage and monitor for system failure due to exhausted RAM
Mint 18 | 3.x | Memory Leak Checks | Frequent equipment polling will take place. No manual GC calls. 4GB RAM will be dedicated to the system. Run for 48 hours; check memory usage and monitor for system failure due to exhausted RAM
OS X El Capitan | 2.7.x | Memory Leak Checks | Frequent equipment polling will take place. No manual GC calls. 8 GB RAM will be dedicated to the system. Run for 48 hours; check memory usage and monitor for system failure due to exhausted RAM
mac OS Sierra | 3.x | Memory Leak Checks | Frequent equipment polling will take place. No manual GC calls. 8 GB RAM will be dedicated to the system. Run for 48 hours; check memory usage and monitor for system failure due to exhausted RAM
Ubuntu 14.04 LTS | 2.7.x | Memory Leak Checks | A Raspberry Pi will be set up and will be used for testing errors. A lot of `set` and `walk` calls will be done on bad OIDs. This will check if PDUs are being released properly in all scenarios. Valid OIDs will also be checked.

Tests will be conducted on Cisco C3560CG, C3750V2, and C3650X models.

Due to time constraints I will be out this weekend, so testing the Pi will be the first to run.

**v0.2.5 will be released no later than June 10th**

issue_title: Remove EvilSessionTools / SparkSession from DataFrameSuiteBase
issue_body:
DataFrameSuiteBase uses [EvilSessionTools](https://github.com/holdenk/spark-testing-base/blob/38cada03f9c34aafea97210957dd32718628f0d7/src/main/2.0/scala/org/apache/spark/sql/EvilSessionTools.scala) to make the [SparkSession accessible](https://github.com/holdenk/spark-testing-base/blob/7191b78ce8160f5dc9e44a3a5751f771bac66848/src/main/2.0/scala/com/holdenkarau/spark/testing/DataFrameSuiteBase.scala#L233-L237) in tests via the `spark` variable.  

I think spark-testing-base users should be able to include DataFrameSuiteBase to access the `assertDataFrameEquals` method and have the flexibility to manage the SparkSession independently of the testing framework.  In my test files, I like to create a SparkSessionTestWrapper trait and extend the spec files to access the SparkSession.

```scala
import org.apache.spark.sql.SparkSession

trait SparkSessionTestWrapper {

  lazy val spark: SparkSession = {
    SparkSession.builder().master("local").appName("spark session").getOrCreate()
  }

}
```

I created a separate testing framework called [spark-fast-tests](https://github.com/MrPowers/spark-fast-tests) to separate the SparkSession dependency from the testing framework.  This approach has a few benefits:

1. spark-fast-tests can easily be used in conjunction with other testing frameworks

2. The user can choose to reuse the SparkSession throughout the entire test suite which is faster (spark-testing-base stops / restarts the SparkSession after every test file)

I think we should either separate the EvilSessionTools stuff to a separate trait or delete it altogether.  Let me know what you think 😄 

P.S. I love this project, studying the codebase has taught me a ton, and I am excited to collaborate and keep making it better!

issue_title: Inconsistent behaviour for gnupghome
issue_body:
When instantiating GPG class from some directory, say ``/tmp`` (other than HOME dir), like this

Documentation says that ``gnupghome`` shouldn't contain any special characters but I guess ``~`` could be expanded while instantiating the class as a special case scenario (I also assume treating ``~`` as a regular character, while working with the library, can't be done easily).

issue_title: template debug should default to DEBUG
issue_body:
Per [docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/topics/templates/#django.template.backends.django.DjangoTemplates) it should default to `DEBUG`. I'm not sure if this is django's fault that it isn't explicitly set somehow, or the plugin's fault that it assumes False.

In any case, if I'm not mistaken, it can be fixed by changing:
`if not template_settings.get('OPTIONS', {}).get('debug', False)`
to:
`if not template_settings.get('OPTIONS', {}).get('debug', settings.DEBUG)`

If `DEBUG` happens to not be in the user's settings file, `settings.DEBUG` defaults to `False`, which seems sensible to me.

issue_title: When do you release the newest version?
issue_body:
Hello

I was using `django-rest-framework-mongoengine==2.0.2` to do something. But I encountered a bug:

```
cannot import name OrderedDict
Request Method:	POST
Request URL:	http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/users/login
Django Version:	1.8.5
Exception Type:	ImportError
Exception Value:	
cannot import name OrderedDict
Exception Location:	D:\INSTALL\Python\lib\site-packages\rest_framework_mongoengine\utils.py in <module>, line 8
```

I tried to track the source code in `rest_framework_mongoengine\utils.py` and I found this code line `from rest_framework.compat import OrderedDict` is the main bug cause. The reason is `rest_framework` hasn't this function, so it definely returned the issue.

Now, I re-download the newest version(2.1.0) from your github, not pip (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/django-rest-framework-mongoengine/2.0.2). I has checked the `utils.py` it hasn't the issue.

Please release fixed version on pip. I believe other developer has same confused. Thanks.

* **Signal** used by **ExportSignal**
* **RelativeTo** used by **SetNamedWaveformNextWritePosition** and **SetWaveformNextWritePosition**
* **HardwareState** used by **GetHardwareState**
* **Trigger** used by **SendSoftwareEdgeTrigger**

(final names for the enums may be different)

group = device.group
device = group.coordinator
service = soco.services.Queue(device)
sub = service.subscribe()
while True:
	try:

		if event.variables:
			pprint(event.variables)

~$ python soco_test.py

```

Don't think it's relevant, but I've commented out a section of data_structures.py (lines 356-362) as follows ...
```
        # Check we have the right sort of element. tag can be an empty string

issue_title: Quick project: give reasonable names to symbolic registers
issue_body:
When a register is accessed (`state.regs.rax`) and nothing was previously written there, angr creates a new symbolic variable and names it something stupid using the VEX register file offset (specifically, something like `reg_10_0_64`). However, here, we have enough information (from `state.arch.register_names` and `state.arch.registers`) to figure out what register was being accessed, and we should really create a name like `reg_rax_0_64` or just `rax_0_64`.

This can be done in: https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/state_plugins/symbolic_memory.py#L438

issue_title: Error Message from S3 misleading when uploading with metadata
issue_body:
when using boto3 version 1.4.5 on Ubuntu 16, If I try to upload a file with user supplied metadata that is non-conformant,  I get an error message back that indicates a different problem:

> S3UploadFailedError: Failed to upload X.zip to X/Xzip: An error occurred (SignatureDoesNotMatch) when calling the CreateMultipartUpload operation: The request signature we calculated does not match the signature you provided. Check your key and signing method.

To reproduce this:
```
import boto3
s3_bucket = 'bucket_name'
s3_key = 'key_name'
filename = 'file_to_upload'
s3_client = boto3.resource('s3')
bucket = s3_client.Bucket(s3_bucket)
obj = bucket.Object(s3_key)
metadata = {
    'key1': 'Value1',
    'KEY1': 'Value1', }
obj.upload_file(filename, ExtraArgs={
                            "Metadata": metadata, })
```

Since S3 metadata keys are all lowercase, the above metadata results in only one key on the S3 side.  That is why the signatures don't match.  It would be clearer if maybe after the error is returned, boto3 checks the metadata it used for case collisions and reports this to the user as well.

issue_title: botocore not honoring AWS_CA_BUNDLE consistently
issue_body:
Perhaps I've misunderstood something, but it appears to me that botocore is not honoring `AWS_CA_BUNDLE` consistently, particularly in its vendored version of Requests. I need this to work because my company intercepts SSL traffic and requires that we use their custom bundle.

First, here's the traceback:

```
The full traceback is:

    caught_exception=caught_exception, request_dict=request_dict)

botocore is being called via Ansible. I am able to run the AWS CLI and other Ansible tasks that use boto3 and legacy boto fine, so I believe my configs are setup correctly. However, the particular task shown above is failing.

Interestingly, if I dig in to my virtual environment, I see this file:

```

If I manually replace this file with the company provided bundle, the SSL errors go away. Somehow, botocore is looking at that file (presumably via Requests) and not at the bundle it should be looking at specified by `AWS_CA_BUNDLE`. If I uninstall certifi I still get the same SSL error.

issue_title: remove code that generates personal access token, instead prompt for "Personal Access Token: " instead of "GitHub Password: " if 2FA enabled
Running 2 web processes in one application
issue_body:
Hi,

I want to build a Rails Web Application that consists of 2 components.
A) A public facing website
B) An internal facing API server

Both will largely work on the same data, so it makes sense for me to have them in one repository because they act on the same database & same Rails models.

I want both components to be seperataly scalable + the internal facing API server to be hosted on seperate machines in my fleet (and having different firewall rules). I know that this last thing is not really yet possible, but ignore that for a second here.

Feature request: make it possible to have 2 (or more) different Procfile entries (web & web_api for example), that both axpose an HTTP frontend. Nice to have would be that they also use seperate set of domain names, although the probably simpler solution would be to expose them on 2 different public ports (web = 80, web_api = 8080), to make sure the proxy routes correctly.

Proposal of a different architecture (eg, using 2 apps) is also welcome, but note that codebase & env settings will be mostly shared...

issue_title: Add intelligent HA app support to the scheduler
issue_body:
Currently Deis exposes no ability to control _where_ containers will be scheduled.  This is often important.  See #1376, which has to do with pinning/pegging a container to a particular host or hosts based on certain arbitrary tags.  (e.g. Run me where there's an SSD.)  This issue is, in essence, a fork of that issue.

Currently, it's possible (and ideal) to run Deis on a CoreOS cluster that spans multiple racks / availability zones / regions / data centers / what have you.  This maximizes the availability of the _cluster itself_, yet there is no way currently to fully take advantage of that and maximize the availability of an _application_ by ensuring its containers are hosted on CoreOS machines in, wherever possible, multiple racks / availability zones / regions / data centers / what have you.

This is a feature request for additional intelligence in scheduling such that this would be possible.

issue_title: Design Doc: Metrics and Telemetry system
issue_body:
This Design Doc is to help implement the following [Open Roadmap Item](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/roadmap/roadmap/#monitoring-telemetry)

## Goals
 * Allow out of box installations of Deis to easily monitor and alert on the state of the cluster.
 * Handle 3rd party integrations for all aspects of the metrics and telemetry system (storage of time series data, graphing, and alerting).

## Code Changes
* Add a drain in the new logger refactor that will send all logs to the Riemann Instance
* Instrument the controller with high level events:
 * App Scaled
 * App Created
 * App Deleted
 * App Released
 * App deployed
 * App rollback
 * Certificate Created
 * Certificate Deleted
 * Key Added
 * Key Deleted

## Tests
 * Will determine this once the scope of changes is determined. Ideally we should start a cluster and run load tests against it and expect to see alerts generated based on that load.

## Glossary
* `Metric` - Can either be a point in time value (amount of heap used) or represent a computational value (request per second, p95 of response time)
* `Event` - Something that occurs within the system that an end user may want to track. For example, scaling containers, exceptions, or a log line. Events **may** contain a metric.

## Requirements
### Collecting metrics and events
Before any metric can be graphed or alert sent data needs to be collected and processed. Passing all of this data through 1 system allows the user to craft rules not only around metrics but also events occurring within the cluster itself. For example:

```
if a deploy has just occurred and load is above 2
  do not alert
end
```

### Metrics and Event Storage
Once the data has passed through the stream processor we will then need to store that data for some amount of time. A time series data base (TSDB) such as influxdb, prometheus, or openTSDB is a good tool for this job. When picking the TSDB that will ship with the Deis installation we will need to keep in mind the operations aspects of running this database in a production environment. 

The datastore **should** support both metrics (gauges, histograms, etc...) and annotations (point in time events) if possible. 

### Alerting
Deis will not ship an alerting system with the cluster but the solution should allow for easy integration with tools like PagerDuty, Nagios, VictorOps, Shinken, and others.

## Proposal 
**The following is the current proposal. It is a living document and will change when new information is gathered or the requirements change.**

### Stream Processor- [Riemann](http://riemann.io)
By adding a stream processor to the set of core Deis components we will provide a central aggregation point for all events, metrics, and alerts. Riemann is a perfect tool for this as it already supports several 3rd party integrations such as InfluxDB, Librato, PagerDuty, Slack, and more. It also has client libraries in most of the popular languages - Go, Java, Clojure, Ruby, Python, and C just to name a few. 

Writing rules in Riemann is pretty simple as the following example shows:

```clojure
(where (and (service "deis-router") (not (state "expired"))) nginx/create-nginx-metrics)
(where (and (service #"deis-router-http-response-code-.*") (not (state "expired"))) (librato :gauge))
```

### Configuration
There are two problems when it comes to configuring Riemann within Deis
 * Generating the core riemann.config file for processing the data stream from the components
 * Generating config files for user apps running on Deis

#### Deis Component Configuration
The core component configuration will be fairly static with the exception for items like metric storage and alerts. Using confd to populate the configurable items should be fairly easy and follows the standard protocol of other components. 

#### User App Configuration
Riemann allows you to include an entire directory of configuration files that it will load in at startup. Users could be provided an interface that allows them to write their configuration file and store it directly within etcd and we use confd to populate files based on those keys. 

### Informant
Informant will send the following information to a configured Riemann Instance:
* Docker Events
* The Docker `/stats` endpoint data for each container
* The `/top` endpoint data for each container

This will satisfy the Host and Container Telemetry requirement for the [Open Roadmap](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/roadmap/roadmap/#monitoring-telemetry).

includes: ['layer:battlemidget/nodejs']

includes: ['lp/adam-stokes/nodejs']
```

issue_title: The websocket connection can fail silently
issue_body:
I don't have a clean repro yet, but I believe this to be the underlying cause of this bug in matrix: https://github.com/juju-solutions/matrix/issues/92

Basically, matrix periodically checks on the "health" of a cluster by checking the status for each unit in each application in Model.applications, where Model is the Model class from python-libjuju's model.py. That data is supposed to be updated by a listener inside the Model object.

If matrix is running on a flaky connection, or if the Controller times the websocket connection out, the listener will stop receiving updates, however, with no Exceptions being thrown. I've verified that this is what's happening by running matrix in a debugger, and checking the .is_open property of the connection. Eventually, it gets set to false because we have been disconnected from the Controller.

We need to either come up with an automagic reconnection scheme, or a recommended pattern for dealing with the case where the listener gets disconnected. 

Note that this is semi-related to https://github.com/juju/python-libjuju/issues/98, though making a Pinger would only address Controller initiated timeouts; a flaky connection would still kill the listener.

issue_title: Unintuitive behaviour with cross_validate.random_train_test_split
issue_body:
The `random_train_test_split` makes is easy to split the `interactions` matrix into train and test dataset but if you have data with weights you will have to apply `random_train_test_split` twice with the same `random_state` parameter. My concern is that it would be intuitive to do something like:
```
from lightfm.data import Dataset
from lightfm.cross_validation import random_train_test_split

users = np.random.choice([0., 1., 2.], (10, 1))
items = np.random.choice([0., 1., 2.], (10, 1))
weight = np.random.rand(10,1)
data = np.concatenate((users, items, weight), axis=1)

dataset = Dataset()
dataset.fit(users=np.unique(data[:, 0]), items=np.unique(data[:, 1]))
interactions, weight = dataset.build_interactions((i[0], i[1], i[2]) for i in data)

test_percentage = 0.2
random_state = np.random.RandomState(seed=1)

train, test = random_train_test_split(
    interactions=interactions,
    test_percentage=test_percentage,
    random_state=random_state
)
train_weight, test_weight = random_train_test_split(
    interactions=weight,
    test_percentage=test_percentage,
    random_state=random_state
)

np.array_equal(train.row, train_weight.row)
np.array_equal(train.col, train_weight.col)
np.array_equal(test.row, test_weight.row)
np.array_equal(test.col, test_weight.col)

>>> False
>>> False
>>> False
>>> False
```
This will result in an incorrect split because the state of the `random_state` changes after the first call to `random_state.shuffle`. For the above example to work as intended you need to make separate but identical `RandomStates`:
```
random_state_interaction = np.random.RandomState(seed=1)
random_state_weight = np.random.RandomState(seed=1)

train, test = random_train_test_split(
    interactions=interactions,
    test_percentage=test_percentage,
    random_state=random_state_interaction
)
train_weight, test_weight = random_train_test_split(
    interactions=weight,
    test_percentage=test_percentage,
    random_state=random_state_weight
)

np.array_equal(train.row, train_weight.row)
np.array_equal(train.col, train_weight.col)
np.array_equal(test.row, test_weight.row)
np.array_equal(test.col, test_weight.col)

>>> True
>>> True
>>> True
>>> True
```

It works but I think it's a little awkward. Two possible solutions/suggestions:
1. Only require a seed parameter and create a RandomState in `cross_validate._shuffle` method. This has the added benefit of fitting in with the larger libraries that only require `seed` and not a `RandomState` generator. I also don't see any additional flexibility by passing in a generator instead of a simple integer.
2. Make a copy of `random_state` before applying shuffle in `cross_validate._shuffle`.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Using pipenv with a mono-repo and subdirectory dependencies and git causes weirdness
issue_body:
I am working on a project that uses a mono-repo and want my Pipfile to have subdirectories in that project as dependencies.

If I lock the following `Pipfile`:
```
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[dev-packages]

[packages]
django = "~=1.11"
"aristotle-cloud" = {path = "./python/aristotle-cloud", editable = false}

[requires]
python_version = "3.6"
```

I get the following entry in my `Pipfile.lock`

```
    "default": {
        "aristotle-cloud": {
            "editable": false,
            "path": "./python/aristotle-cloud"
        },

```

But as soon as any git dependency shows up, for example:
```
[packages]
django = "~=1.11"
django-data-importer = {ref = "master", git = "https://github.com/LegoStormtroopr/django-data-importer"}
"aristotle-cloud" = {path = "./python/aristotle-cloud", editable = false}
```

Locking it now produces this entry in the `Pipfile.lock` file:

```
        "aristotle-cloud": {
            "editable": true,
            "git": "https://github.com/LegoStormtroopr/aristotle-cloud",
            "ref": "ccc9b36237fdde97d68cf4b101ca926dbce2aef8",
            "subdirectory": "python/aristotle-cloud"
        },

```

I'm guessing that its picking up the fact that the file is in a git repo, but this isn't what I want. What I want is to lock the Pipfile to still require the subdirectorys when it is checked out, and install all of the packages in it from that directory, not have it go and try and fetch a different commit and install from there (which in this case it can't because its a private repo).

issue_title: pipenv seemingly not respecting conditional install_requires
issue_body:
## Issue description

When trying to install `ruamel.yaml==0.15.0` using Python 3.6, pipenv tries to build ruamel.ordereddict even though this package is only a dependency for Python 2.7. Interestingly though, pipenv does not attempt to build ruamel.ordereddict when installing `ruamel.yaml==0.15.1`, which changed the way the dependency is defined:

### ruamel.yaml 0.15.0 - [\_\_init\_\_.py](https://bitbucket.org/ruamel/yaml/src/0.15.0/__init__.py)

    install_requires=dict(
        any=[],
        py27=['ruamel.ordereddict'],
    ),

### ruamel.yaml 0.15.1 - [\_\_init\_\_.py](https://bitbucket.org/ruamel/yaml/src/0.15.1/__init__.py)

    extras_require={':platform_python_implementation=="CPython" and python_version<="2.7"': [
        'ruamel.ordereddict',
        ]},

I'm not terribly familiar with python dependencies, but I've never seen them defined as in ruamel.yaml 0.15.0, so I'm thinking that may be the issue.

## Expected result

pipenv not to attempt to build ruamel.ordereddict when running Python 3.6.

## Actual result

pipenv tries to build ruamel.ordereddict, which is an issue since the ordereddict package is not only not required when running Python 3.6, but it doesn't even build :-(.

pipenv --python 3.6

issue_title: Install a package from github, but the generated entry in Pipfile.lock doesn't contain github url
issue_body:
```json
        "host-environment-markers": {
            "implementation_name": "cpython",
            "implementation_version": "0",
            "os_name": "posix",
            "platform_machine": "x86_64",
            "platform_python_implementation": "CPython",
            "platform_release": "4.4.0-104-generic",
            "platform_system": "Linux",
            "platform_version": "#127-Ubuntu SMP Mon Dec 11 12:16:42 UTC 2017",
            "python_full_version": "2.7.14",
            "python_version": "2.7",
            "sys_platform": "linux2"
        }
```

---

[Celery](https://github.com/celery/celery) has a critical bug about timezone, which has been fixed on [master](https://github.com/celery/celery/pull/4173) but not uploaded to [pypi](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/celery).

So have to install celery directly from github:

```shell
$ pipenv install git+https://github.com/celery/celery.git@bd347f7565f3a72c8cfb686ea0bfe38cfa76e09b#egg=celery
# Or
$ pipenv install git+https://github.com/celery/celery.git@master#egg=celery
```

This results an entry in Pipfile:

```
celery = {git = "https://github.com/celery/celery.git", ref = "master"}
```

However, In Pipfile.lock the corresponding entry is like below:

```json
        "celery": {
            "hashes": [
                "sha256:81a67f0d53a688ec2bc8557bd5d6d7218f925a6f2e6df80e01560de9e28997ec",
                "sha256:77ff3730198d6a17b3c1f05579ebe570b579efb35f6d7e13dba3b1368d068b35"
            ],
            "version": "==4.1.0"
        }
```

**So when I do `pipenv install` in another setup, I will get the wrong version of celery.**

See also: https://github.com/celery/celery/issues/4507

issue_title: z-index for .modal

That line must be substituted with   z-index: $zindex-modal;

issue_title: File state incorrectly marked as "errored" if contains chunks is "pending" state
issue_body:
### Description


in `_update` 

```
if cstates.contains_all('finished'):
...
elif cstates.contains_none('running'):              
    self._fstates[parent] = 'errored'
```
If chunks are in `pending` state instead, this will mark the parent state as "errored" which will subsequently cause `active` ( `return not self._fstates.contains_none('pending', 'transferring', 'merging')`) to return `False` and `monitor` to continue with chunks in `pending` / `running` states.

If by the time `run` checks the chunks of a file the chunk has finished, this will continue, if chunks are in `running` this will error.

---

### Reproduction Steps
Not a deterministic way, as it depends on parallel task execution, but either having many chunks or repeatedly will cause this (i.e. we need to 1) have chunks in pending state **and** 2) have some in running state when we check them)

```
def test_dummy(azure):
    def transfer(adlfs, src, dst, offset, size, blocksize, buffersize, shutdown_event=None):
        return size, None

    client = ADLTransferClient(azure, transfer=transfer, chunksize=8,
                               chunked=True)

    client.submit('foo', AzureDLPath('bar'), 32*32)
    client.run()
```
### Environment summary

**SDK Version:** What version of the SDK  are you using? (pip show azure-datalake-store)
Answer here:

Answer here:

issue_title: Make always_yes: true the default?
issue_body:
@srossross and @ilanschnell and I were discussing this the other day. Should we make `alway_yes: true` the default, i.e., conda will not prompt you with yes/no if you want to install a package, unless you explicitly configure always_yes to no? You would still be able to use `--dry-run` to see what would happen without doing it.

The option is very annoying to me personally. always_yes was one of the first things I implemented when I started working on conda and is always the first thing I configure on any new machine that I install conda on. But my experiences shouldn't necessarily be a guide toward understanding the experiences of the typical conda user. I very rarely see people, when I do see them use conda, choose the no option. I do perhaps often see people check what conda will do before choosing yes, although this may just be because the question itself suggests that they need to do this.

issue_title: root environment is always writable
issue_body:
I'm assembling a [Spack](https://github.com/llnl/spack) package for miniconda.

I'd like to treat the resulting installation as "read only" -- in particular, environments installed by users shouldn't end up in the root environment.

This bit in the "envs_dirs" section of [the documentation about centralized installation](https://conda.io/docs/install/central.html) :
> Specify directories in which environments are located. If this key is set, the root prefix envs_dir is not used unless explicitly included. This key also determines where the package caches will be located.

tells me that if I set a value for `envs_dir` then the one in the root prefix will not be used.

But, this .condarc in the root environment directory:

```
envs_dirs:
  - ~/.conda/envs
```

produces this output in `conda info`:

```
...
       envs directories : /home/users/hartzelg/.conda/envs
                          /remote/home/hartzelg/tmp/spack/opt/spack/linux-centos7-x86_64/gcc-4.8.3/miniconda2-4.3.11-vseza2bw4ufem7htzv4jltbvxuzglbmq/envs
```

I've seen other mention that if the root environment is not writable then it is not included in envs directories list.

I have been unable to convince `conda` that the root env. directory is not writable.

I've chmod'ed the root directory itself to `555`.

Then I figured out that it was actually testing `.../conda-meta/history` so I chmod'ed that `444` and for good measure chmod'ed `.../conda-meta` to `555`.

With these changes, the root environment is still reported as writable.

In desperation, I hacked the definition of `root_writable` in `context/base/context.py` to print out some extra info.

```python
    @property
    def root_writable(self):
        from ..gateways.disk.test import prefix_is_writable
        print("in root_writable, self.root_prefix is: %s" % self.root_prefix)
        print("in root_writable, prefix_is_writable(self.root_prefix) is: %s" % prefix_is_writable(self.root_prefix))
        return prefix_is_writable(self.root_prefix)
```

I also added a debug statement to `file_path_is_writable` in `gateways/disk/test.py`

```python
def file_path_is_writable(path):
    log.debug("In file_path_is_writable: %s", access(path, W_OK))
    if isdir(dirname(path)):
```

and finally added a print statement to `cli/main_info.py` just before it dumps out the info:

```python
        print("In info_dict, root_writable is: %s" %info_dict['root_writable'])
        info_dict['_rtwro'] = ('writable' if info_dict['root_writable'] else
                               'read only')
        print("""\
```

Here's the resulting output:

issue_title: Version discrepancy
issue_body:
I wonder why anaconda uses such an old version of Rstudio. I noticed that this version was not even ready for the use with Retina display on the MacBook Pro. So I had to make an extra installation of Rstudio. 

Now about 4 months later and updating to new OS I thought that hopefully a newer version of Rstudio is implemented. But it is still version 1.0153.


I wonder: Am I doing something wrong and it is in fact possible to install the newest version form Anaconda Navigator? And if not, why is Rstudio not updated?

issue_title: use np.expm1 in singlediode model for exp(x) - 1
issue_body:
**Problem**
it is possible that at extremely small voltages there may be machine precision error of less than 1 part per million because the [single diode model](https://pvpmc.sandia.gov/modeling-steps/2-dc-module-iv/diode-equivalent-circuit-models/) has the term `exp(x) - 1` which has an increasing error as x → 0 because `exp(x)` → 1, so 1 - 1 is a very, very small number close to zero.

**Solution**
Use [`np.expm1(x)`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.expm1.html) instead of `np.exp(x) - 1` as suggested in [this comment](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/pull/409#discussion_r199295338)

**Additional context**
#409

issue_body:
Hello,

We are having some troubles with reservation, this is our setup:
In our cluster each worker have 3 network interfaces (3 diferent ip's):
- 10.0.2.X
- 192.168.1.X
- 192.168.201.X

But just with one (192.168.201.X) they can communicate with each other. 

When we execute tensorflowonspark version 1.3.1 reservation process uses the ip in range 10.0.2.x this produces an error.

To solve the problem we have modified get_ip_address function inside util.py to

`def get_ip_address():`
`   """Simple utility to get host IP address."""`
`    return socket.gethostname()`

moving 1.3.1 code to old version (@leewyang https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/issues/251#issuecomment-376337722)

with this little modification all works fine.

But this modification will not work if the nodes can't resolve the name of the workers (lack of DNS or no /etc/hosts configuration)
So. Is there a way to enhance get_ip_address to find the correct ip when the workers have more than one IP?

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: required -m message
issue_body:
I enjoy using the kaggle api, I think it speeds things up for submissions, and I want to make a contribution to it. 

Design wise, I think that the behavior for the -m message option should perform similarly to git. If you don't include the -m option, kaggle api should open up your default editor. By writing and saving the message in the default editor, the message will be passed along with the -m option.

However, a little more investigation leads me to see that Kaggle doesn't interpret new lines in its submission messages. There would be a weird dissonance between your 'commit' message and the actual message you see on kaggle. I think you will see \n text on kaggle instead of actual new lines.

issue_title: Test publish yum repo with different checksums
issue_body:
1. create an "rpm" repo in pulp with one or more RPMs, SRPMs, and DRPMs.
2. publish it with the "sha256" checksum type
3. verify publish (see below)
4. publish it with the "sha1" checksum type
5. verify publish (see below)

### Verification
It depends on how fancy and robust you want to get when it comes to inspecting the published XML. You'll need to somehow look at primary, other, filelists, and prestodelta XML files and make sure all of the unit-related checksums are using the checksum type you asked for.

Smash could download the files and actually parse them as XML. That wouldn't be too difficult, but who likes parsing XML...

Smash could just ensure that the string "sha256" shows up in the case when it should, and doesn't when it shouldn't.

In the case of "other" and "filelists", the checksum type itself won't be included. Each package will have a ``pkgid`` attribute that is the checksum value of the specified type. You could just verify that it's the correct length. Or you could actually cross-reference it with the checksum for the corresponding package listed in primary.

Another general way to help with this verification is to just make sure yum and/or dnf can actually read the metadata and use it. In my manual testing, I published a rhel7 kickstart repo and kickstarted a VM from it, just to make sure it was generally sane.

issue_title: [gltf2] gltf2 export faulting when multiple entities refer to the same mesh
issue_body:
This month I've had problems with glTF2Export faulting on some of my scenes. I've traced the problem to what seems to be memory corruption in glTF2Export::MergeMeshes().

On 20171107, Daniel Hritzkiv submitted commit 9ec117d0bcd97b6e0fc21083c9d995ebc4309527 which modified LazyDict and changed the MergeMeshes code to remove meshes referred by other entities. If I revert that commit, exporting to gltf2 works again (although with the problem that commit was supposed to fix).

My suspicion is that the use of LazyDict::Remove is being too aggressive and corrupting memory. I haven't yet dug into it yet to submit a patch but research is ongoing.

Here is the 4 tasks I need to run, configured in celery.py:

```

issue_title: Tornado Version Issues
issue_body:
From a fresh clone and `conda env update`, `jupyter notebook` gives me the error `'IOLoop' has no attribute 'initialized'`.

This is the same error as in https://github.com/saltstack/salt/issues/46340 so I believe this due to `conda env update` installing Tornado 5, which was very recently released (see issue).

The only way I could fix this was by downgrading to Tornado 4.5.3 (with `conda install tornado=4.5.3`), after which the command works fine.

Creating an issue so someone can look into this more and make any changes that might be needed -- like enforcing tornado version <5. I've seen the issue appearing on the forums today (where there was a potential fix that did **not** work for me personally -- [here](http://forums.fast.ai/search?q=%27IOLoop%27%20has%20no%20attribute%20%27initialized%27))

issue_title: Dependency on old version of colorama lib
issue_body:
fastai python library in its version 0.6 has an indirect dependency (through awscli) on the old version of colorama: <= 0.3.7, current version: 0.3.9). This implies that you cannot install fastai together with h2o, which depends on a disjoint version of colorama (>=0.3.8).

How to reproduce:

```
pip install colorama==0.3.7
pip install fastai
pip install h2o
pip install pipdeptree
pipdeptree --reverse --packages colorama
```

Reverse dependencies of colorama, after installing colorama in the 0.3.7 version required by fastai:
```
Warning!!! Possibly conflicting dependencies found:
* h2o==3.18.0.8
 - colorama [required: >=0.3.8, installed: 0.3.7]
------------------------------------------------------------------------
colorama==0.3.7
  - awscli==1.15.10 [requires: colorama<=0.3.7,>=0.2.5]
    - fastai==0.6 [requires: awscli]
  - h2o==3.18.0.8 [requires: colorama>=0.3.8]

issue_title: async poll/recv_multipart block on last message
issue_body:
I am seeing strange behavior on both the 17.0.0b1 release and on current master (as of commit 5678f964e). Code which works using the existing zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop under 16.0.2 seems to hang on the last message received. In other words, neither recv_multipart() nor poll() yield to notify of the last message on the socket (or so it appears). If additional messages are delivered to the socket, those blocking calls return and progress is made. Strangely, if I put a `yield from asyncio.sleep()` in the receive method and set the zmq.NOBLOCK flag on recv_multipart() (with a try except continue for the empty response), the last message is received and processed.

I also tried swapping the eventloop implementations for both standard asyncio and uvloop, with no change in behavior.

issue_title: `ZMQEventLoop` does not recognize EOF with subprocess pipes (atleast on some Linux)
issue_body:
Code to reproduce:

    import sys
    import asyncio
    import zmq.asyncio
    
    print("libzmq-%s" % zmq.zmq_version())
    print("pyzmq-%s" % zmq.pyzmq_version())
    print("Python-%s" % sys.version)
    
    
    async def subprocess():
        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
            "echo", "hello",
            stdin=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
            stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE)
    
        proc.stdin.close()
    
        print(await proc.stdout.readline())
        print(await proc.stdout.read())
        print(await proc.stderr.read())
        print(await proc.wait())
    
    
    asyncio.set_event_loop(zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop())
    asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(subprocess())

Without the `set_event_loop` line,  I get the expected output:

    libzmq-4.1.6
    pyzmq-16.0.3
    Python-3.6.2 (default, Jul 20 2017, 03:52:27) 
    [GCC 7.1.1 20170630]
    b'hello\n'
    b''
    b''
    0

With it, I get the `b'hello\n'` line. but then nothing more and the CPU is at 100%. Inspecting this, at least on my flavor on Linux `zmq_poll` returns `POLLERR` events for the pipes (because they are closed/at EOF). However, the `ZMQSelector` ignores this event, see `_zmq2aio`.

For this particular issue, treating `POLLERR` like `POLLIN` fixes it, but that might of course have other consequences.

    $ uname -a
    Linux ******** 4.9.51-1-MANJARO #1 SMP PREEMPT Wed Sep 20 10:37:40 UTC 2017 x86_64 GNU/Linux

issue_title: Support CloudFormation intrinsic functions in SAM resources
issue_body:
We quite often use !Ref, !Sub, !Join in properties that are supposed to hold strings such as CodeUri, DefinitionUri, StageName (#19) etc. This is a perfectly valid CloudFormation, but the transformation logic that parses SAM does not resolve intrinsic functions before parsing the property as a string. So the transformation logic would fail and depending on the property it could either return a `validation error` or `internal failure`. Properties that are directly passed down to CloudFormation are unaffected by this problem.

This is a feature request to add support for resolving a subset of [CloudFormation intrinsics](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/intrinsic-function-reference.html) in the transformation logic. We care about only the subset of properties that the transform manipulates, like CodeUri. 

Following intrinsics will be supported:
- !Sub
- !Join

on following properties:

- CodeUri on AWS::Serverless::Function
- Role on AWS::Serverless::Function
- DefinitionUri on AWS::Serverless::Api
- StageName on  AWS::Serverless::Api

issue_labels: [{'id': 563235901, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw1NjMyMzU5MDE=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20csr', 'name': 'area: csr', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 245387015, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyNDUzODcwMTU=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20documentation', 'name': 'area: documentation', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 200596079, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMDA1OTYwNzk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20ui%20/%20ux', 'name': 'area: ui / ux', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 890251927, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4OTAyNTE5Mjc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/current%20sprint', 'name': 'current sprint', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 281347824, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyODEzNDc4MjQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Certbot seems to generate a new private key each time it requests certificates. This makes it very difficult to use [SSL pinning](https://possiblemobile.com/2013/03/ssl-pinning-for-increased-app-security/) with Certbot: ordinarily you would distribute the server (public) key with your application and use the (private) key to obtain the server’s certificate. If the server’s certificate changes every three months then you would also have to update your application every three months (and risk users being locked out entirely if they happened not to upgrade).

The only way to bring your own server key to Certbot [seems to be](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/how-to-use-existing-private-key-with-certbot/17050/2) to create your own CSR and pass this to Certbot using the `--csr` option. (In my understanding the key is “baked in” to the CSR in a way that makes it a suitable proxy for the private key.)

This has drawbacks, however. You have to get the CSR from somewhere but Certbot offers no relevant documentation. (I followed [this documentation](https://github.com/diafygi/letsencrypt-nosudo#how-to-use-the-signing-script) from letsencrypt-nosudo, another ACME client.) More significantly, when Certbot obtains the certificates from your CSR it doesn’t put them in `/etc/letsencrypt` but just in the current working directory. Part of the appeal of Certbot is that it usually puts certificates in a well-defined directory hierarchy; having symlinks to the latest versions in `/etc/letsencrypt/live` is also a boon for convenience. Neither of these is the case when you use your own CSR.

I am requesting that one of the following features be added to Certbot:

1. The ability to point Certbot to an existing server private key, presumably with a command-line flag. (This would be my preferred solution.)
2. Certificates that “came from” a user-provided CSR should be treated the same (i.e. placed in the same directories) as certificates that were obtained in the usual way.

issue_title: Add Sync Flush Support
issue_body:
With Elasticsearch 1.6, a new API and recovery tool called "[Synced Flush](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/1.6/indices-synced-flush.html)" will appear. This will work similar to an optimized index so that indices can be recovered almost instantly, without having to compare segment files. However, the added benefit is that the older indices do _not_ need to be optimized at all; they just need to stop writing.

**Note**: For anyone that happens across this feature request. Optimizing an index is superior to synced flush in that the optimized version will get [sometimes significantly] better search performance, as well as reduced memory.

It would be great if sync flush could be triggered by Curator by having it as an option identical to `optimize`, minus optimize-specific flags (like the number of segments).

```
curator sync-flush indices <<index selection parameters>>
```

It may even be very useful to trigger this before (and perhaps after?) an optimize is run. Optimize is different in that it is just a forced merge that doesn't necessarily imply read-only indices (certainly that's the intent with merging to 1 segment), but synced flushing is meant for read-only (even if just temporary) indices for speedy recoveries. As a result, synced flushes will fail if the shard is being actively written, but the cost is very low.

issue_title: Behaviour for unimplemented messages in messaging spec?
issue_body:
I am currently working on cleaning up how IHaskell handles the communication with Jupyter to be cleaner and more principled.

What is the intended behaviour expected from a kernel when it does not support a given message? For example, the messaging spec has a [history request](https://jupyter-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/messaging.html#history) that IHaskell currently does not implement. What is the _right_ thing for IHaskell to do when it requests a `history_request`?

In these cases, is the right thing to do reply with an empty list? Or what? 

This aspect of the messaging protocol could be documented explicitly in the spec, which would be helpful for future kernel authors.

issue_title: da
Rendering of short mathjax strings next to italics changes based on focus
issue_body:
It looks like there's a subtle bug where something is messed up with the CSS for spacing & italics correction when cells are rendered and unfocused vs. rendered and focused. 

Example:

![mathjax_italics_correction](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2482408/27663240-40aada5c-5c16-11e7-8090-684b8c71fdc9.gif)

```markdown
`————` $m$ *m* $m$ *m* $m$ *m* $m$ *m* $m$ *m* $m$ *m* $m$ *m* `————` Spacing changes 
```


It seems most prominent with letters that are wide when not italic but more narrow when italic (e.g., m) next to short mathematical expressions (such as the individual letter m). 

I imagine that this has to do with simulating the italics correction for math next to italics that from LaTeX handles. But it has to be arising from some difference on our end between the CSS for rendered, focused cells and the CSS for rendered, unfocused cells (since there'd be no reason to disable a mathjax feature for correction on the basis of focus). 

This is probably going to be a pain to figure out and doesn't matter all that much. Still seems worth knowing about as it indicates something weird is going on with our underlying CSS or rendering code.

issue_title: Add capnp serialization to AdaptiveScalarEncoder
State-full Anomaly class - avoid 2 column state fields at T, T-1
issue_body:
currently `Anomaly.compute()` uses 2 main params: `activeColumns` and `predictedColumns`;
there may be some confusion about those variables, but basically `predictedColumns` = output of the TP at time T-1, and `activeColumns` = input to the TP at time T. These 2 values are compared and an anomaly score is produced. 

Enhancement would be to carry one of the states in the class, and require only an actual input. This would simplify things in CLAModel and other user-scripts with anomaly. 

Question is, how this should be called? a `next()` method, or another `compute()` with different params, or even preplace the `anomaly.compute()` as a default?

issue_title: Use add_bytes method of the ipfs client api
issue_body: So far we were using `add` method of the api to add files in the IPFS. As a quick hack we were saving HTTP headers and payload in separate file in a temporary directory then providing the path of that file to the `add` method. This causes unnecessary latency and more importantly, the container directories get unnecessarily added to the IPFS each time a new file is added to them. We can simply use [add_bytes](https://ipfs.io/ipns/QmZ86ow1byeyhNRJEatWxGPJKcnQKG7s51MtbHdxxUddTH/Software/Python/ipfsapi/api_ref.html#ipfsapi.Client.add_bytes) method to directly pass the content in memory without saving it to the disc and getting a reference.

issue_title: Make labels click/hoverable in LZ
Cannot build NN twice with different architectures
issue_body:
What I do:
net = tflearn.input_data(...)
<add layers, eg net = tfl.fully_connected(...)>
model = tflearn.DNN(...)
model.fit(train_data_features, labels)

When I call this the second time, it turns out that the previous graph resides in memory. This problem could be seen in tflearn.models.dnn.__init__() when len(self.inputs)==2 instead of 1. This causes an error later on, when tflearn.models.dnn.fit(...) -> tflearn.utils.feed_dict_builder(...) is called. In this function,  there is a check: 
if len(net_inputs) > 1:
else: X = [X]
The <else> clause applies here, which turns into an error <IndexError: list index out of range> on the following lines
            for i, x in enumerate(X):
                feed_dict[net_inputs[i]] = x
This error occurs due to <X=[X]>

Again, this problem happens due to the Tensorflow graph that resides in memory (the way I see it). To fix it, it is sufficient to modify tflearn.layers.core.input_data(...): just to add the line tf.reset_default_graph() in front of it.
Reference: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/859

issue_title: AttributeError when using custom UserDetailsSerializer
issue_body:
We have created a custom `UserDetailsSerializer` but when trying to run the application we get the error:

`AttributeError: module 'path.to.serializers' has no attribute 'MPUserDetailsSerializer'`

The full error is [pasted here](http://pastebin.com/c6NFpmFF).

The setup for REST_AUTH_SERIALIZERS and USER_DETAILS_SERIALIZER in the django settings is:
```
REST_AUTH_SERIALIZERS = {
    'LOGIN_SERIALIZER': 'questions.serializers.LoginSerializer',
    'JWT_SERIALIZER' : 'questions.serializers.JWTSerializer',
    'USER_DETAILS_SERIALIZER' : 'questions.serializers.MPUserDetailsSerializer',
}
```

The custom serializer is:

```
from rest_auth import serializers as auth_serializers

class MPUserDetailsSerializer(auth_serializers.UserDetailsSerializer):
"""
User model w/o password
"""
class Meta:
    model = User
    fields = ('pk', 'username', 'email', 'name')
    read_only_fields = ('username', )

def validate_email(self, email):
    email = get_adapter().clean_email(email)
    if allauth_settings.UNIQUE_EMAIL:
        if email and email_address_exists(email):
            raise serializers.ValidationError(
                _("A user is already registered with this e-mail address."))
    return email
```

The workaround that we did was to remove the following lines from our local virtualenv, in the file `/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rest-auth/serializers.py`:

```
# Required to allow using custom USER_DETAILS_SERIALIZER  in
# JWTSerializer. Defining it here to avoid circular imports
rest_auth_serializers = getattr(settings, 'REST_AUTH_SERIALIZERS', {})
JWTUserDetailsSerializer = import_callable(
  rest_auth_serializers.get('USER_DETAILS_SERIALIZER', UserDetailsSerializer)
)
```

And replacing `JWTUserDetailsSerializer` with `UserDetailsSerializer` in the same file. But that's a really bad solution since we are changing third parties code and it doesn't make any sense to remove specifically the lines that are told to be required to allow custom `USER_DETAILS_SERIALIZER`, but we don't know what else to do to make it work, are we missing something? a config maybe?

We're using django v1.10.1, djangorestframework v3.4.7 and django-rest-auth v0.9.0

(This issue was firstly created at [stackoverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/41965340/attributeerror-when-using-custom-userdetailsserializer-in-django-rest-auth))

issue_title: Opt-branch, incorrectly returns "infinity"
issue_body:
For the following query:

```
(declare-fun |main::k@2| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::k@3| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::n@3| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::j@2| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::j@3| () Int)
(assert (and
    (<= (* (- 1) |main::k@2|) 0)
    (<= |main::k@2| 1)
    (<= (+ (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) |main::n@3|) 0)
    (= |main::k@3| (+ (- 1) |main::k@2|))
    (= |main::j@3| (+ 1 |main::j@2|))
))

(maximize (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@3|) (* (- 1) |main::k@3|) |main::n@3|))
(set-option :opt.optsmt_engine basic)
(set-option :opt.priority box)
(check-sat)
```

I get the bound "oo".

It is obviously incorrect by looking at the query, but it's even more obvious if we ask Z3 itself whether 
`(= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@3|) (* (- 1) |main::k@3|) |main::n@3|) 10)` (or any number greater than `0` in general) is satisfiable, because the bound should be indeed zero.

What's more interesting, if we drop the constraints `0 <= k@2 <= 1` opt-Z3 correctly returns a bound of `0`.

I bumped in a very strange behavior with z3 4.5.0 with unsat cores.

When I check the following simple formula:
```
(declare-fun p () Int)
(declare-fun q () Int)
(declare-fun r () Real)
(declare-fun ih (Real Int) Int)
(assert (! (not (=> (and (= (to_real (- p 3)) r) (= (to_real q) r)) (or (< p (ih (to_real (- p 3)) (+ q 3))) (<= (ih r p) p)))) :named a2))
(check-sat)
(exit)
```
I correctly get an `unsat` answer from z3. But just by adding the option `(set-option :produce-unsat-cores true)` at the beginning of the file, I get a `sat` answer from the `check-sat` command!

The very same issue arises when using the python API instead of the command line.

issue_title: Unexpected huge performance different between solver using "default" tactic, Z3_mk_solver() and Z3_mk_simple_solver()
issue_body:
I'm currently investigating queries that Z3 is taking a very long time to solve in my tool and I ran into one that Z3 is handling in a surprising way.

This is the query

```
(declare-fun f_ackermann!0 () (_ BitVec 64))
(assert
 (not (fp.isNaN ((_ to_fp 11 53) f_ackermann!0))))
(assert
 (let ((?x8 ((_ to_fp 11 53) f_ackermann!0)))
(let ((?x18 (fp.sqrt roundNearestTiesToEven (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ?x8 ?x8))))
(let ((?x24 (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ?x18 ((_ to_fp 11 53) (_ bv4607092346807469998 64)))))
(let ((?x11 (fp.abs ?x8)))
(let (($x20 (or (fp.isNaN (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ?x11 ((_ to_fp 11 53) (_ bv4607092346807469998 64)))) (fp.isNaN ?x18))))
(let (($x22 (or $x20 (fp.gt (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ?x11 ((_ to_fp 11 53) (_ bv4607092346807469998 64))) ?x18))))
(not (or $x22 (or (or (fp.isNaN ?x11) (fp.isNaN ?x24)) (fp.lt ?x11 ?x24)))))))))))
(check-sat)
```

In my tool libz3 is taking ~1679 seconds to solve this. When I give this query to the Z3 binary it is solving the query in ~2 seconds.

I started digging into this and by making my tool increase libz3's verbosity it looks like the slow `smt` tactic is being used when my tool uses libz3.

```
(smt.searching)
(smt.simplifying-clause-set :num-deleted-clauses 4)
(smt.searching)
(smt.simplifying-clause-set :num-deleted-clauses 30)
...
```

but when Z3 binary solves the query a different tactic is being used.

```
(sat.sat-solver)
(sat.simplify)
(sat-restart :conflicts 202 :decisions 677 :restarts 1 :clauses 185638 :learned 159 :gc-clause 0 :memory 46.75 :time 1.35)
(sat-restart :conflicts 303 :decisions 1100 :restarts 2 :clauses 185638 :learned 246 :gc-clause 0 :memory 46.75 :time 1.36)
...
```

In my tool I use the `Z3_mk_simple_solver()` to create the solver which I expected (maybe this is my mistake?) to have the same performance as the Z3 binary if no explicit tactic is requested and no logic is set. However it seems this is not the case at all.

To investigate some more I tried creating the Z3 solver instance in two different ways in my tool

* replacing `Z3_mk_simple_solver()` with `Z3_mk_solver()`
* Explicitly create the `default` tactic and make a solver from that, i.e. like this
```c++
    theTactic = Z3_mk_tactic(builder->ctx, "default");
    Z3_tactic_inc_ref(builder->ctx, theTactic);
    theSolver = Z3_mk_solver_from_tactic(builder->ctx, theTactic);
```

In both cases these changes to my tool caused performance to improve dramatically on these queries. Looking at the verbose output it seems when I instantiate Z3's solver this way it looks like the much faster `sat.sat-solver` tactic gets used.

So why is this happening? I would have expected `Z3_mk_simple_solver()` to behave like the Z3 binary does when no logic is provided and no tactic is supplied.

To help you reproduce this I'm attaching [z3_interaction.zip](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1018519/z3_interaction.zip) (Z3 interaction log) and 
[solver-queries.core_solver.aa.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1018523/solver-queries.core_solver.aa.smt2.txt) (also a log of queries issued to Z3 in SMT-LIBv2.5 format).

Warning the interaction log has
* Multiple contexts. One is used for solving, the other is just used for logging queries to a text file.
* Sets verbosity to 0

**CC:** @ccadar @danielschemmel
